title: Polish Wycinanki layered cut
subtitle: Cutting and layering symmetrical folk paper designs in the Łowicz tradition
excerpt: Wycinanki (pronounced vee-chee-nahn-kee) is the Polish folk art of paper-cutting, most elaborately developed in the Łowicz region where multiple layers of coloured tissue or craft paper are cut and layered to build up floral and zoomorphic designs. The cuts are made with scissors alone, no knife, producing the characteristic rounded cuts of the hand tradition.
sourceNotes: Marysia Kolodziej, Polish Paper Cutting (1994, Interpress Warsaw). The Łowicz layered-circle composition described here follows Kolodziej's instructions for the 'koguty' (cockerel) format adapted for layered flower rosette.

content.text: Traditional Łowicz wycinanki are built from four to six circles of coloured paper. Each circle is cut with a lacy edge. The circles stack from largest to smallest. The layers give depth and colour contrast. This tutorial builds a five-layer rosette. Each circle is cut with fold-cut symmetry.

items.qty: One A4 sheet of each colour, the design uses circles cut from each sheet
items.name: Coloured craft paper or tissue paper, 5 contrasting colours

items.qty: 1 pair, small nail scissors work well for the inner circles
items.name: Sharp craft scissors with pointed tips

items.qty: 1, the rosette mounts on white for maximum contrast
items.name: White backing sheet, A4

attrs.heading: Materials for one 20 cm Łowicz rosette

content.text: Mark and cut five circles in descending sizes: 20 cm, 16 cm, 12 cm, 8 cm, and 5 cm. Use a different colour for each. Fold each circle into eighths: fold in half, then in half again, then in half once more. The folded wedge will be cut with the folded edges as the symmetry axes.

On the folded wedge, make a series of small V-cuts, semicircles, and triangles along both folded edges and the outer curved edge. Cut into the wedge from the outside but stop 5 mm before reaching the fold lines at the centre, if you cut past the fold the petals will fall apart. Work symmetrically: cuts on the left folded edge should match cuts on the right. Unfold to check; any asymmetry comes from inconsistent cuts on the two sides.

Apply a thin brush of PVA to the centre of the largest circle and place the next circle on top, centred. Rotate each successive layer 22.5° (half a petal width) so the cut edges of each layer show between the edges of the layer above. Press each layer flat with the bone folder. Apply the final (smallest) central circle unrotated, as it will be the visual focus.
